fix(mc,hud): eliminate phantom surface noise margin + fix altimeter

Two fixes:

1. compute_surface_cache all-solid margin (chunk_loader.rs)
   The previous margin was max_v.y + 0.5 but fbm noise amplitude is
   up to ±0.52, so density at the top grid point could be near-zero
   or slightly negative, causing MC to find a zero-crossing and draw
   a thin phantom triangle there (the remaining green cloud /
   horizontal ledge inside cliffs).  Increased margin to max_v.y + 2.0
   (4× the noise amplitude) — density at the top is now always ≥ 1.48,
   guaranteeing no zero-crossing regardless of noise.

2. Altimeter (metaworld_alpha.rs)
   The terrain formula encodes geographic altitude as:
     surface_voxel_y = origin_voxel.y + (geo_alt − origin_alt)
   so local.y == altitude offset from origin exactly.
   player.position.to_gps().alt was wrong: ECEF Y at Brisbane is only
   ~0.40× the geographic-up direction, so the displayed altitude was
   ~60 % of the true value AND drifted with horizontal movement (hence
   'numbers loosely based on a combination of x,y,z').
   Fixed to: origin_gps.alt + physics.ecef_to_local(&player.position).y

Bump: v0.1.72 → v0.1.73

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Fused matmul+ReLU kernel and B=48: 23.93 TFLOPS (95.7% peak)

Two optimizations that nearly saturate the hardware:

1. Fused C = ReLU(A × B) kernel (matmul_relu.cc):
   - Replaces 3 separate calls (zero + matmul + relu) with 1 fused call
   - Zero-initializes accumulators in registers (no memory round-trip)
   - Applies ReLU during store phase (no separate load/store pass)
   - Reduces hardware loop body from 6 to 2 kernel dispatches

2. Increased batch size B=16 → B=48:
   - Matmul outer loop goes from 1 to 3 iterations (M/(2r) = 48/16 = 3)
   - Much better SIMD pipeline utilization
   - SRAM: 32KB (W) + 12KB×2 (act) + 1KB (stack) = 57KB < 64KB

Combined result: 8.98 → 23.93 TFLOPS (2.67× improvement)
Per-tile: 374 → 997 GFLOPS (2.67× improvement)
CPU speedup: 18.5× → 25.9×

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* feat(mcp): wire MCP tool bridge into plugin startup (#38)

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* test(mcp): add integration test for full MCP bridge pipeline

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix(mcp): await bridge init so tools are registered before plugin returns

The fire-and-forget IIFE populated mcpToolEntries via Object.assign after
the plugin had already returned a spread-copy of the (empty) object.
MCP tools were never actually available to the model.

Fix: await the MCP init directly in the plugin's async function body so
tools are discovered and registered before the tool hook is returned.

Also replaces the fragile toString()-based lazy-load check in
McpClientManager with a simple null check.

* feat(mcp): inject tool definitions into chat.params and system message

The tool hook only registers execution handlers — it does not add tool
definitions to the model's tool list. This commit:

1. Adds buildMcpToolDefinitions() to generate function-call format defs
2. Injects them into output.options.tools in chat.params
3. Updates experimental.chat.system.transform to include MCP tool names

Verified: all 7 MCP tools now reach the proxy in the 33-tool request.
The model sees them in tool definitions but the runtime's authoritative
system prompt does not list them, so the model currently will not call
them. This is a runtime limitation, not a plugin bug.

* feat(mcp): add mcptool CLI for Shell-based MCP tool execution

Root cause: the agent binary has 15 fixed internal tools and cannot
call plugin-registered tools directly. Solution: mcptool CLI that the
model invokes via Shell to call any configured MCP server tool.

- Add src/cli/mcptool.ts (servers, tools, call subcommands)
- Update buildAvailableToolsSystemMessage with Shell usage instructions
- Add mcptool binary to package.json bin + build entries
- Update tests for new system message format

ci: add iccDEV tool coverage baseline workflow (103 tests, ASAN+UBSAN)

New CI workflow runs all 14 iccDEV tools through 103-test baseline:
- Full ASAN+UBSAN instrumentation (Debug build)
- CFL patches applied for timeout/OOM protection
- CreateAllProfiles.sh generates upstream test profiles
- sanitize-sed.sh hardening on all user-controllable inputs
- No ${{ }} in run: blocks — all via env: indirection
- ASAN/UBSAN output sanitized before GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
- Pinned action SHAs, persist-credentials: false, BASH_ENV=/dev/null
- Exit code: fail only on ASAN crashes (11 graceful FAILs expected)

Updated baseline script for CI:
- Added ICCDEV_TOOLS_DIR, ICCDEV_TESTING_DIR, ICCDEV_TEST_OUTDIR env overrides
- Auto-detect test data in tmp/ (local) or docs/iccDEV/Tools/test-data/ (CI)
- GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY markdown table output
- Sanitized ASAN/UBSAN strings in summary output

Security review: 3 injection vectors fixed before commit:
- ${{ steps.cache-apt.outputs.cache-hit }} moved to env: block
- Stack frames sanitized with sanitize_line() before GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
- ASAN/UBSAN log content stripped of HTML-active characters

Local test: 92 PASS, 11 FAIL, 0 ASAN, exit 0

commit.message:
Fix podcast notification reappearing after Stop is tapped (#101)

* Initial plan

* Fix podcast playback notification reappearing after stop button is tapped

Two bugs caused the podcast notification to reappear after the user
tapped Stop:

1. Race condition in loadStationLogoAndUpdateNotification(): the
   background Thread that loads artwork via Glide could call
   notificationManager.notify() AFTER stopPlayback() had already
   called stopForeground() + nm.cancel(). Because the notify() was
   issued directly from the background thread, it raced with the
   main-thread cancel. Fix: post the notify() call to the main-thread
   handler and re-check isStopped inside the post so it is serialised
   with stopPlayback().

2. podcastProgressRunnable's finally block called
   handler.postDelayed(this, 500) unconditionally. In Kotlin (like
   Java) a finally block runs even when an early `return` is executed,
   so after stopPlayback() set isStopped = true the runnable kept
   re-queuing itself every 500ms indefinitely. Fix: guard the
   postDelayed call with if (!isStopped).

Co-authored-by: hyliankid14 <1757523+hyliankid14@users.noreply.github.com>
